mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the entire Tudor dynasty (1485-1603) including Henry VII's post-Wars of the Roses consolidation, Henry VIII's English Reformation and establishment of the Church of England, and Elizabeth I's religious settlement and emergence as a Protestant maritime power, directly matching A.5.d.i through A.5.d.iii.

William Cecil shaped the Elizabethan religious settlement, managed Anglo-Spanish relations, and dealt with Mary Queen of Scots, directly covering Elizabeth I's domestic policies and the struggle with Spain under A.5.d.iii.

The Spanish Armada of 1588 represents the climax of Philip II's conflict with Elizabethan England, covering Spanish hegemony under A.5.b.i, Elizabeth's struggle with Spain under A.5.d.iii, and Catholic-Protestant conflicts under A.4.c.

The Thirty Years War (1618-48) and the Peace of Westphalia directly match A.4.d on the end of religious struggles and resecularization of international affairs, A.5.f.ii on German political and religious settlements, and A.5.i on Scandinavian participation.

Maria Theresa's accession triggered the War of the Austrian Succession, her alliance reversal led to the Seven Years' War, and the first partition of Poland occurred under her reign, directly matching C.1.b.i on the Quadruple Alliance and C.2.c.ii on the Pragmatic Sanction.

Louis XIV's reign (1661-1715) directly covers the Age of Louis XIV under C.1.a and C.2.b.ii-iv, including French military campaigns, development of central government, Versailles, Colbert's mercantilist policies, and revocation of the Edict of Nantes.

Frederick the Great's military campaigns in the War of Austrian Succession and Seven Years' War, administrative reforms, and enlightened despotism directly match C.2.d.ii on Frederick II and C.1.b on the emergence of Prussia as a European power.

The Hanoverian Succession of 1714 directly covers the growth of Whig and Tory political parties, the emergence of the cabinet system under George I, and the Protestant succession, matching C.2.a.ii on limited monarchy and the Hanoverian succession.

Mercantilism as the dominant European economic doctrine from the 16th-18th centuries, including Colbert's policies in France and the Navigation Acts in England, directly matches B.4.a.iii on growth of mercantilism and C.2.b.ii on Colbert's mercantilist policies.

The St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre of 1572 represents the climax of the French Wars of Religion, directly matching A.4.c on conflicts between Catholic and Protestant powers and A.5.e.ii on the French Wars of Religion.
The Interregnum (1649-1660) covers the Commonwealth, Cromwell's Protectorate, and radical groups, directly matching C.2.a.i on Oliver Cromwell, the Commonwealth, and the Protectorate.

The Glorious Revolution of 1688 established limited monarchy through the Bill of Rights and constitutional settlement, directly matching C.2.a.i on the end of crown rule without Parliament and C.2.a.ii on limited monarchy under William III.

The Dutch East India Company (VOC) was established in 1602 as a joint-stock company for colonial trade, matching B.4.a.i on development of new business organizations and A.5.h.iii-iv on Dutch commercial supremacy.
The Hanseatic League's decline with the rise of nation-states and Atlantic trade directly matches B.2.a on the decline of the Hanseatic League and shifting trade patterns.

The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth's distinctive political system, religious tolerance, and the three partitions relate to broader European developments, but the detailed coverage belongs in section 962. Include for general context of A.5.h Low Countries connections.
Bohemia's Defenestration of Prague (1618) triggering the Thirty Years' War and the Hussite Wars as a proto-Reformation movement directly match A.4.d and A.5.f.ii on Germany and the Thirty Years' War.

The Industrial Revolution in Britain covers key innovations, the factory system, and enabling factors including colonial trade, directly matching C.2.a.iv on origins of the Industrial Revolution and B.3 on technological advances and pre-industrial manufacturing.

The history of coffee covers coffeehouse culture's relationship to Enlightenment discourse, colonial plantation economies, and its role in global trade, relevant to B.4.a.iv on growth in volume of world trade and C.3.a on origins of the Enlightenment.
The Sack of Rome in 1527 occurred during the Italian Wars amid Charles V's conflicts, directly matching A.4.a on the Italian Wars and A.5.a.i on the French invasion and Spanish defeat of France.

mappings.episodeTitle: The Field of the Cloth of Gold
mappings.rationaleAI: The Field of the Cloth of Gold (1520) between Henry VIII and Francis I represents Anglo-French-Habsburg diplomatic rivalry, directly matching A.4.a-b on the Italian Wars and French-Austrian struggles for European supremacy.

The Franco-American Alliance of 1778 covers France's entry into the American Revolutionary War, France's motivations including revenge for the Seven Years' War, and the financial consequences contributing to the French Revolution, matching C.1.b.ii-iii and C.2.a.iii.

The Covenanters cover the National Covenant of 1638, resistance to Charles I's religious impositions on Scotland, and the Bishops' Wars, directly matching A.5.d.vi on Scotland in the 16th and 17th centuries and the Scottish Reformation.

The Divine Right of Kings doctrine as articulated by James I/VI and challenged by parliamentary sovereignty and Locke directly matches A.5.d.iv on James I's conflicts between crown and Parliament and C.2.a.i on the Glorious Revolution ending crown rule.

The Physiocrats as the first systematic school of political economy advocating laissez-faire against mercantilism directly match C.2.b.v on the reform movement and attacks on economic policies of the ancien regime, and C.3 on the Enlightenment.
Catherine of Aragon's annulment crisis triggered England's break with Rome and the English Reformation, directly matching A.5.d.ii on Henry VIII's divorce question and the English Reformation.

The Grand Tour of the 17th-18th centuries covers the customary educational journey shaping British culture and Neoclassicism, matching C.3.a on the origins of the Enlightenment and cultural developments.

mappings.episodeTitle: The Royal Society and British Science: Episode 2
mappings.rationaleAI: The Royal Society under Newton covers the relationship between scientific knowledge, state power, and navigation during the age of empire, matching A.2 on the Scientific Revolution and C.3.a on the Enlightenment's scientific origins.
The Irish Rebellion of 1798 and its aftermath leading to the Act of Union 1800 directly match C.2.a.v on the growth of Irish patriotism among the Anglo-Irish and Wales in the 18th century.
